title: Iced finger buns
subtitle: Soft white yeasted buns with pink fondant icing
excerpt: Pillowy white enriched buns, long and finger-shaped, glazed with pink icing and sometimes filled with a smear of jam. A British bakery staple with deep roots in the sweet bun tradition.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, describes the plain milk bun from which the finger bun descends. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), documents the iced bun with pink glacé icing as an established British tearoom offering.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze uniced buns on the day they are made. Ice after thawing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape and place on the tray, cover, and prove overnight in the fridge. Bring to room temperature for 45 minutes before baking.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dough made richer with eggs, butter, or milk, which slows fermentation and produces a tender, soft crumb.

content.text: About iced finger buns
Iced finger buns are a fixture in British bakeries and school tuck shops. The bun itself is plain and slightly sweet — a soft
enriched dough

with milk, a little butter, and a small amount of sugar. What makes them recognisable is the shape (long, finger-shaped rather than round) and the thick pink icing spread down the top. The icing is simply icing sugar let down with water and coloured with a drop of pink food colouring, applied once the buns have cooled.

They are an easy first yeasted bake. The dough is straightforward, the shaping requires no special skill, and the prove time is forgiving. Split and filled with a spoonful of strawberry jam, they are better than anything from a bag.
Ingredients
Method
Make the dough

Dissolve {{yeast-dried}} in {{whole-milk}} and leave for two minutes. Mix {{plain-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} together. Add the milk mixture, {{eggs}}, and {{unsalted-butter}} and mix to a soft dough. Knead for seven minutes by hand on a floured surface, or five minutes on a stand mixer with the dough hook, until smooth and elastic. The dough should be soft but not sticky.
Bulk ferment, 60 minutes
Shape into a ball, place in an oiled bowl, and cover with a damp cloth. Leave in a warm place for about 60 minutes until doubled in size.
Shape and prove, 30 minutes

Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface and divide into twelve equal pieces, about 65 g each. Roll each piece under your palm to a sausage shape about 12 cm long. Place the buns spaced apart on two lined baking trays. Cover loosely and prove for 30 minutes until noticeably puffed.
Bake

Heat the oven to 190°C fan (210°C conventional). Bake for 12–15 minutes until pale golden and the buns sound hollow when tapped on the base. Transfer to a cooling rack immediately and leave to cool completely before icing. Icing warm buns makes the glaze run off.
Ice the buns

Mix {{icing-sugar}} with enough cold water to make a thick, spreadable paste — about 2–3 tablespoons. Add one drop of {{food-colouring}} and stir to a uniform pale pink. Spread a thick stripe of icing along the top of each cooled bun. Leave to set for 15 minutes before serving.
Notes
Split the cooled buns along one side and spread with strawberry or raspberry jam before icing. The jam filling is traditional in many parts of England.
Plain flour gives a softer, more tender crumb than strong bread flour in a small bun like this. Strong flour works but the result is chewier.
The icing will crack if you press the buns together once set. Store in a single layer or interleaved with baking paper.
